title: State and Development of GNSS Receiver Usage Worldwide

headers.title: MASS ADOPTION OF GNSS RECEIVERS WORLDWIDE
FORECAST FOR GNSS RECEIVER ACCURACY IMPROVEMENT
FINANCIAL INDICATORS OF THE GLOBAL GNSS RECEIVER MARKET
SHARE OF THE GLOBAL GNSS RECEIVER MARKET IN THE OVERALL GLOBAL MARKET
GNSS RETURN ON INVESTMENT FOR OWNER COUNTRIES

sections.text: This study examines the current state and development forecast until 2035 of GNSS receiver usage worldwide according to five criteria: mass adoption, accuracy, financial market indicators, share of the global GNSS receiver market in the overall global market, and GNSS return on investment.
1 MASS ADOPTION OF GNSS RECEIVERS WORLDWIDE

Let's assess the mass adoption of GNSS receivers worldwide compared to other gadgets, namely smartwatches and smartphones, and the expected growth in GNSS receiver usage and related services over the next 10 years. Table 1 provides a comparative analysis of the mass adoption of GNSS receivers, smartwatches, and smartphones based on multiple sources.
From Table 1, the following KEY CONCLUSIONS AND TRENDS FOR THE NEXT 10 YEARS FOR GNSS RECEIVERS emerge:

items: The number of GNSS receivers will be comparable to the Earth's population. GNSS receivers will penetrate all spheres of human life.

The technical characteristics of consumer and professional GNSS receivers will become similar. However, for professional applications, SLA requirements will remain key and will further develop and spread.

A trend of transition from mass adoption for people to mass adoption for machines. The most significant growth will be driven not by new smartphones, but by billions of new IoT devices: from smart city sensors and agricultural machinery to microchips in postal shipments and animal tracking systems.

Increased accuracy and emergence of new services. Widespread adoption of dual-frequency GNSS chips in consumer devices (already achieved in modern smartphones) will enable centimeter-level accuracy without professional equipment. This will open doors to new services:
• Precise pedestrian navigation indoors and in dense urban environments.
• Augmented reality (AR) with virtual objects anchored to real-world coordinates.
• Robotic delivery and autonomous logistics.
Technology convergence: GNSS receivers rarely work alone. The future lies in their integration with other positioning technologies.

sections.text:
CONCLUSION
While the smartphone and smartwatch market grows by replacing old devices and attracting new users, the GNSS receiver market is poised for a qualitative leap. They will become an integral and "invisible" part of the technological infrastructure managing everything: from autonomous vehicles to smart coffee machines that know your location to brew coffee by the time you arrive.

sections.text: 2 FORECAST FOR GNSS RECEIVER ACCURACY IMPROVEMENT
Improving the accuracy of GNSS receivers, especially using PPP (Precise Point Positioning), is one of the most dynamic and promising prospects for the development of satellite technologies.

It is expected that over the next 5-10 years, there will be a transition from centimeter to millimeter accuracy on a global scale for mass-market applications, and PPP initialization time will decrease from tens of minutes to seconds.
Table 2 shows the current state and a forecast based on multiple sources for improving the accuracy of GNSS/PPP receivers over the next 10 years.
Key drivers that make this forecast realistic:
1. SATELLITE CONSTELLATIONS AND SIGNALS

items: New signals (L5, E5a, E6, L6): Modern satellites transmit more interference-resistant and powerful signals on multiple frequencies. This allows better compensation of ionospheric delays - the main source of errors.
Increasing number of satellites: By 2030, over 150 GNSS satellites will be in orbit.

sections.text: 2. DEVELOPMENT OF PPP SERVICES

items: Classic PPP requires significant convergence time. PPP-RTK is a hybrid technology that combines the global coverage of PPP with the fast convergence of network RTK corrections.

Global network of stations: Companies like Trimble, Hexagon, u-blox, and others are deploying hundreds of base stations worldwide. This allows highly accurate and fast modeling of errors in real-time and distributing corrections via the Internet or satellite.
Delivery via 5G/6G: Next-generation networks will have built-in support for low-latency correction transmission, accelerating PPP initialization to seconds.

Free government PPP services such as HAS (EU) and B2B (China): These are major breakthroughs. Their current accuracy is ~30-40 cm, but by 2025-2027 it promises to reach decimeter level, and in the future, centimeter level.

sections.text: 3. HARDWARE

items: Mass-market dual-frequency chips: If 5 years ago a dual-frequency receiver was an expensive professional item, today such a chip is found in many smartphones. This is the foundation for high accuracy.

Sensor integration: GNSS chips increasingly work in conjunction with inertial measurement units (IMU), odometers, and cameras. In urban canyons or tunnels where GNSS signal is lost, the IMU continues navigation, and when the signal reappears, GNSS quickly corrects the position.

sections.text: WHAT NEW APPLICATIONS WILL THIS ACCURACY ENABLE?

items: For the mass market: Pedestrian navigation indicating the exact entrance or side of the street; precise geotagging of photos and videos; AR games where virtual objects are stably 'attached' to the real world.

For professionals and industry: Autonomous vehicles (cars, drones, delivery robots) capable of precisely determining their lane; robotic agriculture where every centimeter of the field is treated individually; real-time deformation monitoring of buildings and bridges with millimeter accuracy.

sections.text:
CONCLUSIONS
1. The forecast is optimistic. PPP is ceasing to be a niche technology for specialized professional applications and is becoming a standard tool for any task requiring precise positioning.
2. The move toward global, instantaneous, centimeter-level, and accessible accuracy is the main goal of GNSS development for the next decade, and all technological trends point in this direction.

sections.text: LEADING COUNTRIES: TECHNOLOGICAL SOVEREIGNTY AND SALES MARKETS
Leadership here is divided into two categories: GNSS creators (technological sovereignty) and the largest consumers and manufacturers of GNSS components.
1. Technological Leaders (GNSS Creators)
These countries possess the full cycle: from satellites in orbit to solution markets.

items: USA (GPS): Global leader. Dominance in key technologies (chips, military applications), leading number of leading companies (Trimble, Qualcomm, Apple, Google, etc.). Financial flows are highest here.

EU (Galileo): Competitor to the USA. Galileo is inherently civilian, which has opened the market for precise and high-precision navigation services (EGNOS, HAS). Strong positions in geodesy (Hexagon), semiconductors (u-blox, STMicroelectronics), and the automotive industry.

China (BeiDou/BDS): The main 'explosive' player. Made a giant leap by completing the deployment of BDS-3. Huge domestic market (almost all smartphones and cars in China use BDS) and active promotion of the system within the 'Belt and Road' initiative. Chinese companies (Hi-Target, ComNav Technology) dominate the domestic market and are actively expanding internationally.

Russia (GLONASS): Maintains strong positions due to government support, mandatory equipping of transport, and the military-industrial complex. However, the share in the global commercial market, especially in the consumer segment, is low.

Japan (QZSS) and India (IRNSS/NaVIC): Regional systems. Japan leads in autonomous driving and robotics technologies where QZSS is critical. India focuses on the domestic market and neighboring countries.

sections.text: 2. Key Production and Consumption Hubs

items: China, Taiwan, South Korea: Global factories for chips and consumer electronics. Most GNSS chips and modules are produced here.
North America and Europe: Largest markets for PPP solutions (logistics, agriculture, construction) and end consumers of premium devices.

sections.text: LEADING COMPANIES BY MARKET SEGMENT
The leadership picture varies significantly by segment (Table 4).

Trimble, Hexagon, Fugro, John Deere (leader in agri-segment), Sapcorda (joint venture Bosch, Geo++, u-blox)
The most promising segment. Battle over whose cloud correction service becomes the standard for robots and autonomous systems.

sections.text:
KEY TREND
The main financial and technological trend: Value is shifting from hardware (chips are getting cheaper) to services and data. Future profits will be derived not only from selling receivers but also from monthly subscriptions for centimeter accuracy (PPP services), location-based analytics, and platform solutions.

CONCLUSIONS
1. The global financial market is gigantic and will continue to grow at an accelerating pace.
2. The USA-EU-China trio dominates technological and financial leadership, with China increasing its influence most rapidly due to its colossal domestic market and government support.
3. Among companies, American technology giants 'rule' the consumer segment, while American Trimble and European Hexagon lead in the high-precision PPP sector.

The share of the global GNSS receiver market in the overall global market currently and for 2035 (forecast) according to multiple sources is shown in Table 5.

sections.text:
CONCLUSIONS
1. Relative share: The financial relative share of the global GNSS receiver market in the overall global market (0.24%) is enormous.
2. Growth dynamics: It is clear that the growth rate of the GNSS receiver market (~140%) exceeds the forecast growth of many other large markets (except cloud computing).
3. Changing position: In 2024, the GNSS receiver market is a 'catching up' player. By 2035, it will not only catch up but also significantly approach the size of giants like the smartphone and semiconductor markets.
5 GNSS RETURN ON INVESTMENT FOR OWNER COUNTRIES

sections.title: Table 6
sections.description: Comparative assessment of GNSS return on investment (USA, EU, China, Russia)
rows: Return on investment (excess of revenues over costs)

sections.text:
1. Company profits:
   • Calculated as 20% of cumulative revenue over all years (standard profitability for the high-tech sector).
   • Example for the USA (by 2024): (80+110)/2 * 20% * 15 years ≈ 150 billion $.
2. Government revenues (taxes):
   • Calculated as 20% of company profits (average effective corporate tax burden).
   • Example for the USA (by 2024): 150 billion $ * 20% = 30 billion $.
3. Indirect economic effect:
   • Cumulative effects over all years of system operation (efficiency improvements in logistics, agriculture, energy, telecommunications) are considered.
   • The figures are estimates and show a multiple excess over direct costs.
4. Payback year: Calculated as the point at which cumulative tax revenues + government share of indirect effect (1-2%) exceeded cumulative system costs.

KEY CONCLUSIONS:
1. Systems have likely already paid off in all countries. Investments have brought colossal returns in the form of taxes and overall economic growth.
2. China demonstrates the highest payback speed and the largest cumulative indirect effect due to total integration of the system into the national economy.
3. In Russia, payback has been achieved not through direct commercial profit, but through indirect socio-economic effect.
KEY TRENDS AND CONCLUSIONS FROM THE FORECAST

1. The GNSS industry has firmly established and is developing leading positions in the world.
2. In all countries, their GNSS systems are and will remain infrastructure critical to national security and sovereignty. HOWEVER
3. GNSS technologies have transformed from purely military to an integral part of every Earth inhabitant's life.
4. Shift in commercialization leadership:
   • 2024: USA and China share first place in commercial returns.
   • 2035: China will break ahead as leader due to total integration of the system into its economy and expansion into Asia, Africa, and the Middle East.
5. Growth of indirect effect: For all owner countries, the indirect contribution of GNSS to GDP will be orders of magnitude higher than both direct system costs and direct revenues from GNSS receiver sales. This confirms GNSS's status as critical infrastructure.
